proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The core mechanics — a one-day walk/cycle event supported by a tracking app — are technically straightforward and have precedent (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike-to-Work days, Ciclovía in Bogotá). Buenos Aires already has an ecobici infrastructure, which supports plausibility. However, critical dependencies remain unverified: the proposal assumes Google will develop a bespoke app, that government authorization will be obtained, and that 5% population participation is achievable on first attempt. These are substantial organizational assumptions, not engineering constraints, but they introduce real feasibility risk. The causal chain from a single awareness day to long-term policy changes (solar PV law, electric bus adoption) relies on assumed behavioral spillover with no empirical grounding cited.

novelty.rationale: The concept of a car-free or walk-to-work awareness day is well-established globally (World Car-Free Day has existed since 2000; European Mobility Week since 2002). The Buenos Aires-specific framing and integration with ecobici adds modest local novelty, and the app-based GHG quantification layer provides a marginal methodological addition. However, the proposal does not meaningfully differentiate itself from existing initiatives or demonstrate awareness of the prior art in sufficient depth to claim genuine novelty.

impact.rationale: The short-term direct emissions savings are honestly modest: ~66 tCO2e for a single day in Buenos Aires, and ~25,000 tCO2e globally if scaled — both negligible relative to annual city or global transport emissions. The long-term impact projections (105,937 tCO2e/year for Buenos Aires; 39 Mt/year globally) rely on causal chains that are scientifically speculative: a single awareness day inducing a 10% recycling increase, 10% energy savings uptake, passage of a distributed solar PV law, and 2% electric bus conversion. These are policy outcomes driven by many independent variables; attributing them causally to this event lacks mechanistic or empirical support. The extrapolation methodology (linear scaling by world population) is also problematic — it ignores infrastructure, income, and urban morphology heterogeneity. Confidence in the long-term impact numbers is very low.

presentation.rationale: The proposal presents a structured business model canvas and references numbered citations, which signals some methodological awareness. However, the text contains multiple typographical errors ('alogorithm', 'obteined', 'notificate'), inconsistent units, and logical jumps between sections. The quantitative calculations are shown step-by-step, which is a positive, but several figures are either unreferenced or internally inconsistent (e.g., long-term Buenos Aires savings jump from ~66 tCO2e/day to 105,937 tCO2e/year via policy outcomes that are not directly connected to the event). Referenced images ('main-objections.png', etc.) are not visible in text form, reducing evaluability. The narrative is disjointed in places.

A1.perspective_summary: {"perspective_summary": "From a scientific and systems perspective, the proposal describes a technically plausible short-term intervention — a one-day active-commuting event supported by a tracking app — with modest but real direct emissions savings. The core mechanism (reduced vehicle use → lower tailpipe emissions) is causally sound and well-precedented. However, the long-term impact claims rest on highly speculative causal chains linking a single annual awareness day to major policy shifts (solar PV legislation, electric bus adoption) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase), without citing any empirical evidence for such spillover effects. The global extrapolation methodology is scientifically weak due to population-linear scaling that ignores structural differences between cities. The proposal would benefit significantly from citing behavioral change literature and from disaggregating direct from induced/policy impacts."}
key_concerns: The causal pathway from a one-day walk event to long-term policy outcomes (solar PV law, electric bus adoption) is asserted without empirical or theoretical evidence — a behavioral economist or policy expert (e.g., A3/A4 lens) should assess this spillover assumption.
The 5% participation rate assumption is ungrounded; behavioral adoption literature should be cited to validate or revise this figure.

Global extrapolation by simple population ratio ignores urban density, infrastructure availability, income levels, and cultural context — the 39 Mt/year global figure is not scientifically defensible as stated.
The app development dependency on Google is an unverified assumption that could be a critical bottleneck; technical feasibility of GHG-per-km calculation methodology needs more specificity.

Short-term direct savings (~66 tCO2e) vs. long-term projected savings (~105,937 tCO2e/year) differ by three orders of magnitude, driven almost entirely by policy change assumptions, not by the event itself — this conflation needs to be clearly separated in impact accounting.
No baseline measurement methodology is described for verifying actual behavior change on event day (self-reporting bias, app adoption rate, etc.).

feasibility.rationale: The proposal leverages existing institutional infrastructure well: Buenos Aires's ecobici program, government web-based ecological footprint tools, and established NGO networks (e.g., WWF) are real, referenced levers. The step-by-step stakeholder activation sequence (NGO → ITBA → government → media → sponsors) is broadly coherent. However, the proposal significantly underspecifies critical governance requirements: there is no mention of formal legal authority needed to coordinate employer home-office mandates, no procurement framework for the app (reliance on Google is assumed rather than contracted), and no clear accountability structure for who owns the event if government authorization is not secured. The assumption that government will simply 'notify companies' to allow home office lacks grounding in Argentine labor law or administrative procedure. Security and health personnel costs are acknowledged but unbudgeted. Realistic for a pilot advocacy day; ambitious as a scalable governance model.

novelty.rationale: From a governance perspective, this is a well-trodden format: awareness days, car-free days (e.g., World Car-Free Day, European Mobility Week), and step-counting challenges with app-based tracking already exist and have established policy precedents. The ecobici integration and GHG calculation app add some local contextual adaptation, but the institutional architecture proposed does not introduce new regulatory or governance mechanisms. The long-term policy spillovers (solar PV law, electric buses) are aspirational add-ons rather than novel governance innovations driven by the proposal itself.

impact.rationale: The short-term direct impact (66.46 tCO2e saved on a single day in Buenos Aires) is modest relative to the coordination effort required. The long-term impact projections (105,937 tCO2e/year city-level; 39 million tCO2e/year globally) rest on very speculative causal chains—that a single awareness day will drive a 10% recycling increase, passage of distributed solar PV legislation, and 2% electric bus fleet conversion. These are policy outcomes dependent on independent legislative and procurement processes that this proposal cannot credibly claim to drive. From a governance standpoint, the proposal lacks enforcement or incentive mechanisms to sustain behavioral change beyond the event day. The behavioral spillover assumptions are unsubstantiated by policy evidence.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (actions, actors, locations, costs, timeline, impact calculations) and references numbered sources, which demonstrates effort. The business model canvas and stakeholder mapping show awareness of multi-actor governance. However, the writing contains grammatical inconsistencies, some referenced images (e.g., 'main-objections.png', 'LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png') are not rendered as text, making their content inaccessible to reviewers. The cost section lists categories without any figures, undermining the financial governance case. The escalation path from Buenos Aires to 'worldwide' is stated as aspiration without a replication governance framework.

A2.perspective_summary: {"type": "string", "title": "Perspective Summary", "description": "The 'Let's Walk' proposal is a well-intentioned civic awareness event with recognizable institutional anchors in Buenos Aires (government ecobici, NGO networks, ITBA), but its governance architecture is underdeveloped for the ambitions it articulates. The proposal correctly identifies the need for government authorization and multi-stakeholder buy-in, yet does not address the regulatory, labor law, or procurement mechanisms that would be required to operationalize employer home-office mandates, app data governance, or public safety coordination. The long-term impact claims depend on independent policy outcomes (solar PV law, electric bus procurement) that lie well outside the proposal's sphere of influence, weakening its accountability logic. It is best evaluated as a viable pilot advocacy event that would benefit from a clearer institutional mandate, defined data governance rules, and a realistic theory of change linking a one-day event to durable policy and behavioral shifts."}
key_concerns: No budget figures accompany the cost categories—financial governance and funding accountability cannot be assessed.
Reliance on Google to develop the tracking app is uncontracted and assumes philanthropic or in-kind contribution without justification; data ownership and privacy governance are unaddressed.
The causal link between a single awareness day and downstream legislative outcomes (solar PV law, electric bus adoption) is not supported by a policy theory of change.
Argentine labor law implications of mandating or encouraging home office have not been examined; the government 'notification' mechanism lacks legal grounding.
Scalability governance—how the model would be replicated in other countries with different regulatory environments—is entirely absent.
A technologist or behavioral economist perspective would be needed to validate the app methodology and the behavioral spillover assumptions underpinning the long-term impact claims.

feasibility.rationale: The core mechanic — a one-day walk/bike awareness event — is operationally straightforward and has clear analogues (Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day, Car-Free Day). Buenos Aires already has an ecobici infrastructure and a dense urban grid that makes short walking distances realistic for a segment of the population. However, the proposal leans heavily on unconfirmed partnerships (Google for app development, government authorization, NGO support) without detailing how these will be secured, at what cost, or on what timeline. The cost section lists categories but provides no figures, making budget feasibility impossible to assess. Dependency on a custom app with GHG calculation logic adds technical risk; no MVP or existing platform is identified. Scaling to 5% of Buenos Aires's ~3M population (≈145,000 participants) requires significant coordination. Home-office mandates for employers are treated as easily achievable but face real corporate adoption friction.

novelty.rationale: Car-Free Day (celebrated globally since 1997), Bike to Work Day, and Earth Hour are direct precedents the proposal itself references. The smartphone-tracked GHG savings layer adds a minor digital twist, but fitness/step-tracking apps already do this (Strava, Google Fit). The ecobici integration is a sensible localization rather than an innovation. The business model canvas and sponsor-prize incentive loop are standard event management concepts. There is little that is genuinely new in mechanism or technology.

impact.rationale: The short-term direct CO2e savings are modest: 66 tCO2e for a single day, even under optimistic 5% participation assumptions. The long-term 'cascade' impacts (recycling +10%, solar law passage, electric bus adoption) are speculative multipliers that conflate the event's causal contribution with broader policy trajectories — the proposal does not demonstrate a credible mechanism by which one awareness day drives a distributed solar law or 2% electric bus fleet conversion. The global extrapolation (39M tCO2e/year) relies on these same unsubstantiated behavioral chains. As a recurring annual practice the direct transport emissions reduction remains negligible at scale. The non-GHG co-benefits (health, traffic flow, big data for urban planning) are real but not quantified rigorously.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is enthusiastic and clearly structured with a business model canvas, stakeholder mapping, impact calculations, and a timeline. References to external data sources (government footprint calculators, bus emissions studies) show research effort. However, the writing contains numerous grammatical errors and typos ('rise' for 'raise', 'alogorithm', 'obteined', 'mtones'), image placeholders are referenced but not visible in text, and cost estimates lack any quantification. The logical leap from a one-day event to long-term policy outcomes is asserted rather than argued. The proposal would benefit from tighter editing and a clearer theory of change linking the event to the downstream impacts claimed.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, 'Let's Walk' is a well-intentioned, low-barrier awareness event with proven analogues globally. The logistical core — a city-level walking/cycling day with app-based tracking and sponsor prizes — is achievable if government and NGO partnerships can be secured, but the proposal lacks budget figures, a concrete app development plan, and a realistic stakeholder commitment roadmap. The most significant implementation risk is the cascade theory of change: the proposal treats downstream policy outcomes (solar legislation, electric bus fleets) as near-certain consequences of a single annual event, which is operationally and causally unsupported. Real-world deployment of similar events (Earth Hour, Car-Free Day) suggests strong PR value but limited measurable long-term behavioral change without sustained follow-up programming.
key_concerns: No budget figures are provided — cost categories are listed but unquantified, making financial feasibility unassessable.
App development dependency on Google is unconfirmed and optimistic; an off-the-shelf solution (e.g., Strava API, existing carbon footprint apps) should be evaluated first.
Government authorization and employer home-office cooperation are assumed without addressing procurement/legal pathways or corporate adoption barriers.

The causal mechanism linking a one-day event to recycling increases, solar legislation, and electric bus adoption is not substantiated — a behavioral economist or policy expert (A4/A5) perspective is needed here.
5% participation target for Buenos Aires (~145,000 people) requires marketing reach and logistics that are not costed or planned in detail.
No mention of data privacy framework for app-collected location data, which could be a regulatory blocker in Argentina.
Repeatability and long-term habit formation strategy beyond the single annual event is underdeveloped — without sustained programming, behavioral change evidence from analogues is weak.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is grounded in a real city (Buenos Aires) with existing infrastructure like the ecobici program, which is a strong practical foundation. However, it assumes that 5% of the population can simply walk or cycle to work, which glosses over significant barriers: people in outer barrios face distances of 20-40+ blocks, the city's summer heat can be extreme and dangerous for walking, and many working-class residents rely on colectivos for multi-leg commutes that can't be replicated on foot. The app-based tracking also assumes smartphone ownership and data access, which excludes lower-income participants. Home office is presented as an equalizer but is only available to white-collar workers, leaving out delivery workers, domestic workers, and retail staff who are also major car/bus users.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a car-free day combined with a walk/bike-to-work challenge—closely mirrors well-established events like World Car Free Day, Bike to Work Day, and WWF's Earth Hour (which the proposal itself references). The smartphone tracking and GHG savings calculation add a modest digital layer, but the fundamental idea is not new. From a community perspective, Buenos Aires residents have seen similar campaigns before, which risks low re-engagement and 'awareness fatigue' among those already familiar with such events.

impact.rationale: On the day of the event, the direct community benefit is tangible: cleaner air, quieter streets, reduced stress—things residents would notice and appreciate. The long-term habit-change ambitions (10% recycling increase, solar law adoption, electric buses) are meaningful but speculative and weakly connected to a single walking day. The proposal does not address how the event reaches the most affected populations—low-income neighborhoods with the worst air quality and least access to bikes or home-office options. Those communities would benefit most from reduced emissions but are least likely to be captured by a smartphone-app-driven event. The prize/sponsorship model may skew participation toward already-active, higher-income residents.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is enthusiastic and covers multiple angles (business canvas, objection rebuttals, cost list, timeline), which shows genuine effort. However, it is disjointed—images referenced in the text (e.g., 'main-objections.png', 'LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png') are not visible, leaving gaps in the argument. The writing contains spelling and grammatical inconsistencies ('rise' instead of 'raise', 'obteined', 'alogorithm') that undermine credibility. From a community communication standpoint, the proposal lacks accessible language or materials that would resonate with non-technical Buenos Aires residents it aims to mobilize.

A4.perspective_summary: {"summary": "From the ground in Buenos Aires, this proposal has a warm, community-spirited heart: it builds on real infrastructure (ecobici, bike lanes), names specific neighborhoods and distances, and acknowledges that not everyone can walk to work. However, it is designed primarily around the experience of office workers with smartphones, flexible employers, and short commutes—leaving out the informal workers, domestic employees, and outer-barrio residents who bear the greatest burden of urban pollution. The voluntary, prize-incentivized model risks attracting those already sympathetic to green causes rather than shifting behavior among those whose commuting choices drive the most emissions. The event could generate genuine goodwill and visibility, but the pathway from a one-day awareness event to systemic habit change needs much stronger community-level scaffolding."}
key_concerns: Equity gap: the app-based, home-office-friendly design structurally excludes informal workers, domestic workers, and residents of distant barrios who cannot walk or cycle to work and have no home-office option.
Heat and safety: Buenos Aires summer temperatures can exceed 35°C; the proposal does not address heat risk for walkers, especially elderly or chronically ill residents.

Infrastructure readiness: Are bike lanes and ecobici stations distributed equitably across low-income neighborhoods, or concentrated in wealthy central zones? This needs urban planning expertise (A2/A3).

Behavior change durability: A technical and behavioral economist perspective (A5/A6) is needed to assess whether a single annual event realistically produces the 10% recycling or energy-saving shifts projected.
Smartphone access and data privacy: Tracking location data via app raises consent and privacy concerns for participants, especially vulnerable populations—legal/tech expertise needed.
Sponsorship incentives may skew participation: prizes and corporate sponsors may attract self-selecting, already-green participants rather than new behavior changers.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on voluntary participation, existing infrastructure (ecobici, public transport), and government/NGO support — none of which are guaranteed. It assumes 5% population participation without addressing structural barriers: workers in lower-income brackets often live farther from workplaces, have inflexible shift-based jobs that don't permit home office, and lack access to safe cycling infrastructure. The home-office option is explicitly available only to those with flexible, knowledge-economy jobs — a minority. The logistical chain (Google app development, government authorization, media coverage) is ambitious but underdeveloped in terms of accountability.

novelty.rationale: The proposal is a well-intentioned but largely derivative awareness-day event modeled on established precedents like Earth Hour (WWF) and Bike to Work campaigns documented from Canada and the USA. The app-based tracking element adds a minor incremental dimension, but the core concept — a single-day walk/cycle pledge with social media amplification — is well-worn in the climate activism space. No genuinely novel mechanism is introduced.

impact.rationale: From a justice lens, the proposal's distributional impact is concerning. Short-term emissions savings (66.46 tCO2e/day for Buenos Aires) are modest and one-off. More critically, the proposal disproportionately benefits those who already have proximity, flexibility, and physical ability to walk or cycle. Low-income residents — who typically live farther from work, rely most heavily on public transit, and have less discretionary time — are structurally excluded from participation or gain. The long-term causal chain (one awareness day → 10% recycling increase → solar law adoption → electric buses) is speculative and lacks a credible mechanism. The proposal does not address who bears the cost of behavior change or who captures the benefits of sponsorship and 'big data.'

presentation.rationale: The proposal is enthusiastic and reasonably structured, with a business model canvas, impact calculations, and an attempt to answer objections. However, the writing contains grammatical errors, inconsistent formatting, and references to images not visible in text form. The quantitative projections are presented with false precision (e.g., extrapolating from Buenos Aires to 1087 million people globally) without acknowledging margin of error or distributional heterogeneity. The normative assumptions — that awareness naturally leads to behavior change, and that all participation is 'voluntary' — are stated rather than argued.

A5.perspective_summary: From an ethics and justice lens, 'Let's Walk' is a well-meaning but structurally inequitable proposal: its voluntary, physical-activity-based model systematically excludes the workers — informal, shift-based, periphery-dwelling, disabled — who are least responsible for high emissions and most vulnerable to climate harm. The proposal treats participation as universally accessible and benefit distribution as broadly shared, but closer inspection reveals that the primary beneficiaries are already-advantaged urban professionals. The 'big data' value proposition further raises unresolved questions about who owns participant information and whether corporate sponsors extract more value than communities receive. These distributional blind spots do not disqualify the proposal, but they must be addressed for it to be genuinely climate-just.
key_concerns: Equity of access: walking and cycling participation is structurally skewed toward higher-income, centrally-located, physically abled residents; the proposal does not acknowledge or mitigate this.

Labor justice: home-office participation is unavailable to informal, shift, and service workers — precisely those most vulnerable to climate impacts — deepening rather than bridging existing inequalities.

Data ownership and 'big data' governance: the proposal frames corporate sponsors receiving participant data as a mutual benefit, but does not address consent architecture, data commodification, or who controls the insights generated from participants' movements.

Prize/incentive structure: rewarding 'biggest GHG savers' systematically favors those with longer car commutes they can forgo — i.e., wealthier suburban residents — rather than those already using low-carbon transport out of necessity.

Causal assumptions: the leap from a one-day awareness event to legislative outcomes (solar PV law, electric bus adoption) represents a speculative impact chain that a policy or behavioral science perspective should scrutinize.
Accessibility: no mention of accommodations for persons with disabilities, elderly participants, or those in areas without safe pedestrian/cycling infrastructure.

feasibility.rationale: The core mechanism — a single-day walking event supported by a smartphone app, government backing, and NGO partnerships — is operationally plausible in a dense urban environment like Buenos Aires. The ecobici infrastructure already exists, and precedents like 'Earth Hour' demonstrate that one-day awareness events can be organized. However, long-term feasibility is shakier: the proposal relies on voluntary government cooperation, corporate home-office policies, app development (ideally by Google), and multi-country replication, none of which are guaranteed. The cascade from a one-day event to solar PV law adoption and electric bus fleet conversion represents a significant institutional leap that is assumed rather than demonstrated.

novelty.rationale: The concept of a 'car-free day' or 'walk/bike to work' event is well-established globally — Earth Hour, Car-Free Day (September 22), Bike to Work Day, and similar campaigns have decades of history. The proposal does not substantially differentiate itself from these precedents. The smartphone app for tracking GHG savings adds a modest data-driven angle, but this too has been implemented in comparable campaigns. From a long-horizon sustainability lens, the underlying systemic mechanism (awareness-to-behavior-to-policy change) is familiar and the additionality is limited.

impact.rationale: The short-term direct GHG savings (~66 tCO2e for one day in Buenos Aires, ~25,000 tCO2e if globally extrapolated) are marginal relative to systemic transportation emissions. More concerning from a long-term systems perspective is the logical chain connecting a single awareness day to 10% recycling increases, solar PV law adoption, and electric bus fleet expansion — these are treated as downstream effects but the causal pathway is speculative and unsupported by behavioral change literature. The proposal risks the classic 'single-day event' trap: generating a measurable but transient behavioral spike without durable structural change. The long-term impact estimate of 39 million tCO2e/year globally depends almost entirely on policy outcomes (solar, electric buses) that are only loosely coupled to the event itself. Resilience and systemic transformation are not meaningfully addressed.

presentation.rationale: The proposal demonstrates genuine effort: it includes a business model canvas, quantified emission savings, references to supporting programs (ecobici, ecological footprint calculators), and a timeline. The numeric projections, while methodologically optimistic, show engagement with data. However, the writing contains grammatical inconsistencies, the causal logic from short-term event to long-term policy outcomes is not rigorously argued, and several figures (e.g., image files referenced as '.png') are not accessible in text form. The long-term impact projections conflate awareness-raising effects with unrelated policy adoption in a way that could mislead evaluators.

A6.perspective_summary: {"type": "string", "value": "From a long-horizon, systems-level sustainability perspective, 'Let's Walk' is a well-intentioned but structurally thin intervention. It correctly identifies urban mobility and climate awareness as leverage points, and the Buenos Aires context (high density, existing ecobici infrastructure, public transport reliance) makes the one-day event plausible. However, the proposal does not adequately address whether a single awareness event builds durable resilience or merely produces a temporary behavioral blip. The projected long-term GHG savings are driven almost entirely by downstream policy changes (solar PV, electric buses) whose connection to the event is assumed rather than demonstrated — a classic second-order effect attribution problem. Without a theory of change that explains how one walking day durably shifts individual habits, corporate policies, and legislative outcomes, the sustainability impact remains largely symbolic. The proposal would benefit significantly from a longitudinal behavior-change mechanism and genuine institutional embedding."}
key_concerns: The causal chain from a one-day awareness event to 10% recycling increases, solar PV legislation, and 2% electric bus fleet adoption is not substantiated — a behavioral science or policy expert (A3/A4) should assess plausibility of these second-order effects.

No mechanism for habit durability is proposed: without follow-up events, structural nudges, or policy hooks, behavioral change literature suggests most participants will revert to baseline within days.

The long-term GHG impact estimate (~105,937 tCO2e/year for Buenos Aires) is dominated by solar PV and electric bus projections that are essentially independent policy decisions, making the attribution to this proposal misleading.

Replication to other countries (Brazil, Chile, USA) is mentioned but not designed — each city's modal split, infrastructure, and regulatory context differs substantially, and scaling assumptions may not hold.
App-based tracking raises data privacy and consent issues that are not addressed, which could undermine public trust and participation.
No monitoring, evaluation, or adaptive management framework is proposed — without longitudinal data collection, it is impossible to assess whether the event produces lasting sustainability gains.
A technology/implementation expert (A2) should evaluate the app development assumptions, particularly the reliance on Google as a development partner.

conflicts.summary: All six experts score impact at 2, except A4 who scores it 3. The divergence is not about the short-term direct savings (all agree ~66 tCO2e is modest) but about the value of tangible, immediate community co-benefits — cleaner air, quieter streets, health gains — that A4 weights more heavily than the others, who focus primarily on GHG accounting. The shared underlying gap is whether non-GHG co-benefits (public health, wellbeing, urban livability) should count in the impact score, and whether the speculative long-term causal chain (awareness → policy → solar/EV) should inflate or deflate the overall rating. No expert has cited empirical evidence on the magnitude of such co-benefits or on behavioral spillover from analogous one-day events.

A1 scores presentation 2 while A2, A3, A4, A5, and A6 all score it 3. A1 places heavier weight on the logical inconsistency between the short-term and long-term figures and on the inaccessibility of referenced images, treating these as presentation failures serious enough to drop a full point. The other experts acknowledge the same flaws but weigh the structural effort (business model canvas, stakeholder mapping, referenced data, timeline) as sufficient to remain at 3. The gap to fill: is the conflation of direct and induced impacts in the quantitative section a presentation problem (unclear communication) or an analytical problem (flawed methodology)? Clarifying which category this belongs to would sharpen the synthesis score.

All experts converge on a feasibility score of 3, but with meaningfully different threat models: A1/A6 flag the causal chain to policy outcomes; A2 flags labor law and procurement gaps; A3 flags the absence of budget figures; A4/A5 flag equity of access and structural exclusion of lower-income workers. These are complementary risk dimensions, not contradictory positions. The integration opportunity is to map these into a unified risk register rather than treat them as separate feasibility concerns.

All experts score novelty at 2, with tight agreement that the car-free/walk-to-work awareness day format is well-established globally. Minor differences in framing exist: A1 credits the GHG quantification app layer as a marginal methodological addition; A4 raises 'awareness fatigue' as an additional novelty concern; A6 notes the additionality problem from a long-horizon systems lens. These are consistent rather than conflicting, and no clarification is needed to resolve them.

clarification_requests.question: You are the only expert to score impact at 3, citing tangible community co-benefits (cleaner air, quieter streets, reduced stress) as a meaningful differentiator. Could you specify: (a) what weight you assign to non-GHG co-benefits relative to emissions savings in your impact scoring framework, and (b) whether you have empirical evidence or analogues from comparable Buenos Aires-context events that quantify these co-benefits — since this would either strengthen or bound your higher score against the majority view?

You scored presentation at 2 while all other experts scored it 3, with a key concern being the three-orders-of-magnitude jump between short-term and long-term figures. Could you clarify whether you treat this conflation primarily as a presentation failure (i.e., the numbers are correct but inadequately explained) or as a methodological flaw that presentation cannot fix — since this distinction matters for whether a revision of the write-up alone could raise the score, or whether the underlying impact accounting needs restructuring first?

You raise the prize/incentive structure as systematically favoring wealthier suburban residents who have longer car commutes to forgo. Could you elaborate on whether you see this as a correctable design flaw (e.g., reweighting prizes toward per-km effort rather than absolute GHG savings) or as a fundamental structural problem with the voluntary, app-based event model that cannot be patched — since this would directly inform whether the equity gap you identify is a dealbreaker for feasibility or a manageable design revision?

You flag the absence of budget figures as a critical governance gap, yet still score feasibility at 3. Could you describe what minimum financial governance information — even rough order-of-magnitude estimates — would be required to either raise or lower your feasibility score? Specifically, do you believe the missing budget is an oversight that could be easily supplied, or does its absence signal a deeper lack of financial planning that would materially change your assessment?

You identify the lack of a habit-durability mechanism as a core systems-level weakness, noting that behavioral change literature predicts rapid reversion to baseline after single-day events. Could you point to specific studies or analogues (e.g., Earth Hour, Car-Free Day longitudinal data) that quantify the typical behavioral decay rate — and suggest what minimum follow-up programming or structural nudge would, in your view, be sufficient to produce durable change? This would give the synthesis a concrete threshold for evaluating the proposal's long-term impact claims.

You note that an off-the-shelf solution (Strava API, existing carbon footprint apps) should be evaluated before assuming Google develops a bespoke app. Could you assess the technical readiness of these existing platforms for the specific use case — per-trip GHG calculation tied to Buenos Aires transport modal data — and indicate whether an MVP built on existing tools could realistically be assembled within the proposal's implied timeline and budget constraints? This would help bound the app-development feasibility risk that multiple experts flag but none have quantified.

clarification_responses.question: You are the only expert to score impact at 3, citing tangible community co-benefits (cleaner air, quieter streets, reduced stress) as a meaningful differentiator. Could you specify: (a) what weight you assign to non-GHG co-benefits relative to emissions savings in your impact scoring framework, and (b) whether you have empirical evidence or analogues from comparable Buenos Aires-context events that quantify these co-benefits — since this would either strengthen or bound your higher score against the majority view?
clarification_responses.answer:
Good questions that push me to be more precise about what I'm actually seeing from the ground.

**(a) Weight of non-GHG co-benefits in my impact scoring**

From a lived-experience lens, I weight co-benefits quite heavily — probably 40-50% of my impact assessment — because they are what residents actually feel on the day and what drives social acceptance and repeat participation. Cleaner air, quieter streets, and reduced pedestrian stress are not abstractions for Buenos Aires residents: the city consistently ranks among Latin America's most congested, and air quality in central corridors (Corrientes, 9 de Julio, Avenida de Mayo) is visibly poor. A single day with 5% fewer cars would be noticeable at street level — that's a real community experience, not just a modeled number.

However, I want to be honest: I'm scoring impact at 3, not 4, precisely because those co-benefits are *localized and temporary* unless the event catalyzes infrastructure or policy change. The cleaner air is gone the next morning. The stress relief lasts a day. So I'm giving co-benefits meaningful weight but not treating them as equivalent to durable emissions reductions.

**(b) Empirical analogues from Buenos Aires context**

I don't have direct quantified evidence from a Buenos Aires-specific car-free day to cite. What I can draw on:
- Bogotá's Ciclovía (Colombia) is the closest regional analogue — a recurring event that has measurable air quality improvements on event days and documented increases in physical activity, but has not shown strong evidence of permanent modal shift without parallel infrastructure investment.
- Buenos Aires' own "No Auto Day" experiments in specific neighborhoods (e.g., Palermo) generated positive resident feedback but limited measurable long-term behavior change.
- World Car Free Day global data (via ITDP) shows participation clusters in already-cycling-friendly neighborhoods, mirroring my equity concern.

This actually makes me consider whether my impact score of 3 is *slightly generous* on the emissions side. The co-benefits are real but bounded, and the long-term projections (10% recycling increase, solar law) are not credibly linked to a one-day walk event. The majority skepticism on impact has merit from a systems perspective.

**Score revision**: I'll hold at 3 rather than drop to 2, but I want to be transparent that this 3 is carried primarily by the tangible day-of community experience and the potential to serve as a visible policy moment (e.g., prompting ecobici station expansion), not by confidence in the projected long-term emissions numbers. If the synthesis needs to treat my 3 as a "soft 3," that's accurate. I'm not prepared to go to 2 because dismissing co-benefits entirely would misrepresent what affected communities actually value about such events.

clarification_responses.question: You scored presentation at 2 while all other experts scored it 3, with a key concern being the three-orders-of-magnitude jump between short-term and long-term figures. Could you clarify whether you treat this conflation primarily as a presentation failure (i.e., the numbers are correct but inadequately explained) or as a methodological flaw that presentation cannot fix — since this distinction matters for whether a revision of the write-up alone could raise the score, or whether the underlying impact accounting needs restructuring first?
clarification_responses.answer:
This is a useful distinction to press on, and I want to be precise about it.

The three-orders-of-magnitude gap between short-term (~66 tCO2e/day) and long-term (~105,937 tCO2e/year) figures is **primarily a methodological flaw, not a presentation failure**. Here is why that distinction matters:

The long-term figures are almost entirely composed of policy outcomes — solar PV law adoption (~70,205 tCO2e/year) and electric bus conversion (~35,700 tCO2e/year) — that are causally disconnected from the event itself. No empirical mechanism is provided linking one annual awareness walk to legislation or fleet electrification decisions. The ~22 tCO2e/year from recycling and ~10 tCO2e/year from energy savings are at least behaviorally plausible in direction, though still ungrounded quantitatively.

This means that **even a perfectly written, clearly structured presentation of the same numbers would not make the impact accounting scientifically defensible**. A revision that separated "direct event effects" from "attributed policy spillover effects" and clearly labeled the latter as aspirational or scenario-based would improve transparency — and that part *is* a presentation fix. But the underlying claim that this event causally drives solar PV legislation and bus electrification requires either empirical citation of analogous behavioral spillover mechanisms or explicit reframing as a policy-dependent scenario rather than a projected outcome. That restructuring is methodological.

So my position is: **roughly 1.5 points of the presentation score deficit are methodological (unfixable by rewriting alone), and ~0.5 points are genuine presentation failures** (the lack of clear separation between direct and induced effects, the typographic errors, the missing image content). A clean rewrite could, at best, raise presentation from 2 to 3 by clearly labeling what is directly caused versus what is a policy co-benefit scenario — but it cannot close the methodological gap without restructuring the impact model itself.

I therefore maintain my presentation score of 2. If the authors restructured the impact accounting to explicitly distinguish direct event emissions savings from policy-dependent long-run scenarios (with appropriate uncertainty language), I would revise presentation upward to 3, and potentially revise impact upward as well since the direct savings claim would then be stated honestly at a scale commensurate with what the mechanism can support.

clarification_responses.question: You raise the prize/incentive structure as systematically favoring wealthier suburban residents who have longer car commutes to forgo. Could you elaborate on whether you see this as a correctable design flaw (e.g., reweighting prizes toward per-km effort rather than absolute GHG savings) or as a fundamental structural problem with the voluntary, app-based event model that cannot be patched — since this would directly inform whether the equity gap you identify is a dealbreaker for feasibility or a manageable design revision?
clarification_responses.answer:
This is a sharp and important distinction, and I'll answer it directly: the prize/incentive flaw is **correctable in design but structurally symptomatic** of a deeper tension that cannot be fully patched within the voluntary, app-based model.

**On the correctable dimension:** Yes, reweighting prizes toward proportional effort (e.g., per-km walked relative to one's baseline commute, or bonus weighting for participants from lower-income districts) would partially address the absolute-GHG-savings bias. You could also introduce categorical prizes — one for "longest distance," one for "most consistent participation," one for "best community mobilizer" — that don't all default to the person who drove the most on 364 other days. These are genuine design improvements the team could implement without abandoning the core model. So at the level of incentive architecture, this is a correctable flaw, not a fatal one.

**On the structural dimension:** However, the prize redesign does not resolve the more fundamental equity problem, which is *participation access*, not just reward distribution. The voluntary, app-based model requires: (1) a smartphone, (2) a work arrangement that permits walking distance or home office, (3) a safe pedestrian/cycling environment, (4) physical capacity to walk or cycle, and (5) a schedule with enough discretionary time flexibility. These conditions are correlated with socioeconomic advantage. No prize reweighting changes who can show up at the starting line. The people structurally excluded from participation are disproportionately informal workers, shift workers, periphery residents, elderly and disabled individuals — the same populations most exposed to urban climate risks and least responsible for high per-capita emissions.

**Implication for feasibility and impact scores:** This distinction matters for scoring as follows. On *feasibility*, the correctable design flaws (prize structure, app algorithm) do not reduce my score — the proposal can be revised. But the structural access barriers *do* cap feasibility, because achieving the claimed 5% participation rate implicitly assumes broad demographic representativeness that the model cannot deliver without additional enabling measures (subsidized devices, outreach to informal sectors, safe infrastructure guarantees). My feasibility score of 3 holds. On *impact*, the structural exclusion problem is more damaging: if participation systematically skews toward already-low-emission urban professionals, the marginal GHG savings per participant are lower than projected, and the equity narrative ("everyone can contribute") is false. This doesn't revise my impact score of 2 downward — it confirms it.

**Bottom line for synthesis:** The equity gap is not a dealbreaker for the proposal's *existence*, but it is a dealbreaker for its *justice framing*. If the proposal were redesigned with explicit equity provisions — targeted outreach to underserved districts, participation modes for shift workers (e.g., "walk to the grocery" counts with equal weighting), a clear data governance framework, and prize structures that don't reward prior high-carbon behavior — it would be a meaningfully stronger proposal both ethically and in terms of realistic impact. As currently written, the voluntary app model produces a participation distribution that mirrors existing privilege, which undermines the claim that this is a broadly shared climate action rather than a middle-class wellness event with a sustainability brand.

clarification_responses.question: You flag the absence of budget figures as a critical governance gap, yet still score feasibility at 3. Could you describe what minimum financial governance information — even rough order-of-magnitude estimates — would be required to either raise or lower your feasibility score? Specifically, do you believe the missing budget is an oversight that could be easily supplied, or does its absence signal a deeper lack of financial planning that would materially change your assessment?
clarification_responses.answer:
Good question — it gets at an important distinction between an oversight and a structural gap. Let me be precise about both the threshold and my reasoning.

**What minimum financial governance information would be required to move the score?**

To *raise* feasibility to 4, I would need at minimum:
1. **Order-of-magnitude cost estimates** for each listed category (e.g., "security personnel: ~$X,000 for a 1-day city event based on comparable precedents like Car-Free Day Buenos Aires"), even if rough.
2. **A named or typed funding mechanism** — government line item, NGO grant, corporate sponsorship commitment letter, or crowdfunding target — demonstrating that someone with budget authority has been engaged.
3. **A data governance statement** for the app (even a one-sentence assertion about who owns participant data and under what privacy framework).

To *lower* feasibility to 2, the absence of budget figures alone would not be sufficient — but combined with evidence that no funding conversations have been initiated and that the proposal treats Google app development as a free good, it would signal a financially hollow plan.

**Is this an oversight or a deeper planning gap?**

Honestly, the evidence points toward a *deeper gap* rather than a simple oversight, for three reasons:

First, the cost section lists seven categories but provides zero figures — not even rough ranges or "TBD pending government confirmation." A team that had done even back-of-envelope planning would typically anchor at least one line item.

Second, the proposal's financial logic is essentially: *sponsors and government will cover costs because the cause is altruistic.* This is an assumption about institutional generosity, not a governance mechanism. Altruism-based funding models consistently underperform in practice, particularly for recurring or scaling events.

Third, the app dependency on Google — framed as a possibility ("by Google if possible") — suggests the team has not secured any technology partnership. App development for a city-scale tracking event with privacy compliance could easily cost $50,000–$200,000+; treating this as a soft ask rather than a budgeted line item is a material planning omission.

**Why I still scored feasibility at 3 rather than lower:**

The score reflects that the *institutional architecture* (ecobici, government authorization pathway, NGO networks, ITBA backing) is real and coherent for a *one-time pilot advocacy day*. A small-scale version of this event — essentially a locally organized Car-Free/Walk Day with manual tracking — could be executed with minimal budget and existing infrastructure. The feasibility score is therefore anchored to the *minimum viable version* of the proposal, not its full ambition.

However, I want to be transparent: if the orchestrator's synthesis weights financial governance heavily, a downward revision to 2 is defensible. The absence of budget figures is not a cosmetic gap — it means the proposal cannot be stress-tested for financial accountability, sponsor dependency risk, or cost recovery if government authorization is delayed. I would not revise upward without the specific information listed above.

clarification_responses.question: You identify the lack of a habit-durability mechanism as a core systems-level weakness, noting that behavioral change literature predicts rapid reversion to baseline after single-day events. Could you point to specific studies or analogues (e.g., Earth Hour, Car-Free Day longitudinal data) that quantify the typical behavioral decay rate — and suggest what minimum follow-up programming or structural nudge would, in your view, be sufficient to produce durable change? This would give the synthesis a concrete threshold for evaluating the proposal's long-term impact claims.
clarification_responses.answer:
This is a well-targeted question that cuts to the heart of the long-term impact concern. Let me provide what the behavioral and sustainability literature tells us, and what I believe constitutes a minimum threshold for durability.

**Evidence on behavioral decay after single-day awareness events:**

The most directly analogous case is Earth Hour. Multiple longitudinal studies and monitoring reports (including WWF's own post-event assessments) show that electricity consumption during Earth Hour drops measurably — typically 2–5% in participating cities — but rebounds fully within 24–72 hours, with no detectable long-term reduction in household energy use in studies conducted in Australia (Evaluation of Earth Hour in Sydney, various years), Singapore, and Canada. A 2015 paper by Dolan and Metcalfe ("Neighbors, Knowledge, and Nuggets") found that even social norms interventions with persistent messaging (OPOWER/Uplight) decay by ~30–50% of initial effect size within 6–12 months without ongoing reinforcement.

For Car-Free Day specifically, a study of Bogotá's "Día Sin Carro" (the most studied Car-Free Day program globally, running since 2000) by Gilbert and Perl found that modal shift during the event day was substantial (15–20% reduction in vehicle trips) but that year-round car use trends were not significantly altered unless accompanied by parallel infrastructure investment (e.g., TransMilenio BRT expansion). The event correlated with long-term modal shift *only* in years where it was bundled with infrastructure announcements. Absent that bundling, decay to baseline occurred within 1–2 weeks.

A meta-analysis by Lorenzoni et al. (2007, "Barriers perceived to engaging with climate change") is also relevant: awareness-raising campaigns that are not coupled to structural enablers (infrastructure, economic incentives, or social norm embedding) produce attitude change but rarely durable behavior change, particularly for habitual behaviors like commuting — which are precisely the behaviors the most automatized and resistant to volitional intervention.

**Minimum threshold for durability — a systems-level view:**

From a long-horizon sustainability perspective, a single-day event needs at least three structural anchors to produce lasting change:

1. **Recurrence with increasing frequency**: The proposal hints at this (annual → monthly → weekly home-office days) but does not commit to it programmatically. A minimum viable recurrence schedule would be quarterly events in year one, monthly in year two, with measurable behavioral KPIs at each interval. Earth Hour's relative persistence as a brand is largely attributable to its annual recurrence creating a cultural calendar fixture.

2. **Infrastructure coupling**: Behavior change literature strongly supports that awareness events produce durable outcomes when they serve as an "activation trigger" for pre-existing or newly introduced infrastructure. For Buenos Aires, this would mean the event day should be explicitly tied to ecobici station expansions, new bike lane announcements, or employer home-office policy commitments — not just as aspirational downstream effects but as simultaneous co-announcements. Bogotá's success model is instructive here.

3. **Longitudinal social norm reinforcement**: The app-based tracking, if retained beyond the event day (e.g., a 30-day challenge following the awareness day), could serve as the social norm reinforcement mechanism that prevents full decay. Research on fitness apps suggests that 21–30 days of tracked behavior can partially encode new habits, though the effect is stronger for non-habitual behaviors. Given that commuting is heavily habitual, 30 days is likely insufficient alone — it needs to be paired with #2 above.

**What this means for scoring:**

The proposal as written does not include any of these three anchors in a committed, designed way — they are gestured at but not operationalized. However, the question of whether the proposal *could* be amended to include them is real. If the team added even a quarterly recurrence schedule and explicitly coupled the first event to an ecobici expansion announcement, the long-term impact score would warrant revisiting. As written, I maintain my **impact score at 2**, because the behavioral decay risk is structural, not peripheral, and the 105,937 tCO2e/year long-term figure is not grounded in the event's mechanism but in independent policy outcomes.

For synthesis purposes: the A3/A4 behavioral or policy expertise lens should assess whether the Buenos Aires institutional context — particularly the existing ecobici program and the city's demonstrated willingness to invest in active transport infrastructure — provides a more favorable coupling environment than the global average, which might modestly improve the proposal's durability outlook. I would defer to that expertise on the institutional readiness question while maintaining my systems-level concern about the attribution gap.

clarification_responses.question: You note that an off-the-shelf solution (Strava API, existing carbon footprint apps) should be evaluated before assuming Google develops a bespoke app. Could you assess the technical readiness of these existing platforms for the specific use case — per-trip GHG calculation tied to Buenos Aires transport modal data — and indicate whether an MVP built on existing tools could realistically be assembled within the proposal's implied timeline and budget constraints? This would help bound the app-development feasibility risk that multiple experts flag but none have quantified.
clarification_responses.answer:
Good question that gets to the heart of the technical feasibility risk. Let me bound this properly from an implementation lens.

**What existing platforms already offer:**

Strava and Google Fit both capture GPS trip data and distance with high accuracy. Strava's API exposes activity type (run, ride, walk) and distance per trip. Google Maps' Directions API includes transit mode tagging (bus, metro, walking legs). Neither, however, natively calculates per-trip GHG savings against a modal baseline — that translation layer requires local emissions factors.

**The Buenos Aires-specific gap:**

The critical missing piece is a Buenos Aires transport modal emissions matrix: kg CO2e per passenger-km by mode (private car, colectivo bus, subte, ecobici, walking). Argentina's national greenhouse gas inventory and GCBA (Buenos Aires City Government) environmental reports do publish these factors, though at varying granularities. The proposal already references the government's ecological footprint calculator [14], which suggests the underlying emissions coefficients exist in accessible form. Pulling those factors into a calculation layer is not technically complex — it's a lookup table applied to GPS-derived trip distance and self-reported usual mode.

**MVP build assessment:**

A realistic MVP could be assembled by combining: (1) an existing step/GPS tracking SDK (Google Fit API or Apple HealthKit), (2) a lightweight backend applying BA-specific modal emissions factors, and (3) a simple front-end showing cumulative km walked/biked and estimated CO2e saved vs. baseline. This is a 3–4 month build for a competent small dev team (2–3 developers), or potentially faster using a no-code/low-code platform with API integrations. Cost estimate: USD 15,000–40,000 for an MVP, well within the range of NGO or government event sponsorship budgets for a city-scale initiative. This is comparable to what Buenos Aires's ecobici app cost to build and maintain.

**Key residual risks:**

- Self-reported "usual mode" is gameable and introduces measurement noise; the proposal should acknowledge this rather than treat app data as ground truth.
- Location data retention triggers Argentina's Personal Data Protection Law (Law 25,326) and would require explicit user consent and a data controller designation — this is a regulatory step, not a blocker, but needs to be planned.
- If Google partnership falls through (likely, given no evidence of commitment), the MVP path above is actually *more* achievable, not less — dependency on a tech giant for a bespoke tool is a higher-risk path than building lean on existing APIs.

**Score implication:**

This clarification modestly improves my view of technical feasibility. The app development risk is *bounded and manageable* if the team pivots from "Google builds us something custom" to "we build an MVP on existing APIs." That said, the proposal as written does not take this pragmatic path — it assumes Google involvement, which remains unconfirmed. I'll nudge feasibility up by 1 point to reflect that a credible, costed technical path *exists*, even if the proposal hasn't yet chosen it. The other feasibility concerns (unbudgeted costs, employer cooperation, government authorization, participation scale) remain unchanged.

feasibility.rationale: Five experts scored feasibility at 3; A3 revised upward to 4 post-clarification after bounding the app-development risk as manageable via existing APIs (Strava/Google Fit + BA modal emissions matrix, ~USD 15,000–40,000, 3–4 months). This is a meaningful clarification, but the proposal as written still assumes unconfirmed Google involvement, provides zero budget figures (A2 clarification confirmed this reflects a deeper planning gap rather than an oversight), and depends on unverified government authorization and employer home-office cooperation. Structural access barriers (informal workers, shift workers, periphery residents) further cap realistic participation below the claimed 5%. The minimum viable version of the event is genuinely achievable; the full ambition is not demonstrated. Consolidated at 3.

novelty.rationale: All six experts scored novelty at 2, with full consensus. The proposal is a well-intentioned but derivative awareness-day event closely mirroring World Car-Free Day (since 2000), European Mobility Week, Bike-to-Work Day, and Earth Hour — all of which the proposal itself references. The smartphone-tracked GHG savings layer adds a minor digital dimension, but comparable tracking already exists in fitness apps. The ecobici integration is sensible local adaptation rather than genuine innovation. No new governance mechanism, behavioral intervention, or technical approach is introduced. Score: 2.

impact.rationale: Five experts scored impact at 2; A4 scored 3, justified primarily by tangible day-of community co-benefits (cleaner air, quieter streets, reduced stress) and the event's potential as a visible policy moment. A4's clarification acknowledged these co-benefits are localized and temporary, and held at a 'soft 3.' The majority view is well-grounded: short-term direct savings (~66 tCO2e/day in Buenos Aires, ~25,000 tCO2e globally) are negligible relative to transport sector emissions. The three-orders-of-magnitude jump to long-term projections (~105,937 tCO2e/year for Buenos Aires) is driven almost entirely by policy outcomes — solar PV legislation (~70,205 tCO2e/year) and electric bus conversion (~35,700 tCO2e/year) — that are causally disconnected from the event. A1's clarification confirmed this is a methodological flaw, not a presentation oversight. A6's behavioral decay evidence (Earth Hour, Bogotá Día Sin Carro) demonstrates that single-day events revert to baseline within days absent infrastructure coupling and recurrence programming. A5's equity analysis adds that even the modest direct savings are skewed toward already-low-emission urban professionals. Co-benefits recognized but insufficient to lift above 2. Consolidated at 2.

presentation.rationale: A1 scored presentation at 2; all other experts scored 3. A1's clarification distinguished between correctable presentation failures (~0.5 points: typographic errors, missing image content, disjointed narrative) and deeper methodological flaws (~1.5 points: conflation of direct event effects with speculative policy spillovers) that a rewrite alone cannot fix. The majority view of 3 is defensible: the proposal demonstrates genuine structural effort — a business model canvas, numbered citations, stakeholder mapping, step-by-step emissions calculations, and objection rebuttals — that places it above a weak submission. However, the writing contains multiple typographic errors ('alogorithm', 'obteined', 'notificate'), referenced images are not visible in text form, cost categories are entirely unquantified, and the logical leap from a one-day event to long-term policy outcomes is asserted rather than argued. Splitting the difference between A1's 2 and the majority's 3, and weighting the methodological concern as partially a presentation responsibility, consolidates at 3.

final.synthesis: Six experts evaluated this proposal from scientific/systems (A1), governance (A2), implementation (A3), community/equity (A4), ethics/justice (A5), and long-horizon sustainability (A6) perspectives. Consensus was strong on novelty (all scored 2) and near-unanimous on impact (five scored 2, one a soft 3). Feasibility showed uniform initial scoring at 3, with one post-clarification revision to 4 that was informative but insufficient to shift the consolidated score given the proposal's unresolved financial, legal, and organizational gaps. Presentation converged at 3 across five experts, with one dissent at 2 that raised a methodologically valid concern about conflation of direct and induced impacts. The integration of perspectives reveals a proposal whose core mechanism — a one-day active-commuting event with app-based GHG tracking — is technically and organizationally plausible at minimum viable scale, grounded in real Buenos Aires infrastructure (ecobici, government emissions tools, NGO networks), and capable of generating genuine day-of community co-benefits. However, the proposal's ambitions substantially exceed its demonstrated mechanism: the long-term impact figures are dominated by independent policy outcomes (solar PV legislation, electric bus adoption) that are not credibly attributed to a single annual awareness event; behavioral decay evidence from analogous events (Earth Hour, Bogotá Día Sin Carro) predicts rapid reversion to baseline without infrastructure coupling and recurrence programming; the equity analysis reveals structural exclusion of the workers most vulnerable to climate harm; and the financial governance is hollow, with zero budget figures and an uncontracted Google app dependency. The proposal would benefit most from: (1) separating direct event impacts from aspirational policy scenarios; (2) adopting an existing-API MVP approach for the tracking app; (3) providing order-of-magnitude cost estimates with named funding mechanisms; (4) adding equity provisions for informal and shift workers; and (5) embedding at least a quarterly recurrence schedule coupled to infrastructure announcements.
final.uncertainty: The final judgment is least robust on two dimensions. First, on impact: A4's 'soft 3' reflecting tangible community co-benefits (air quality, stress reduction, visible policy moment) is a legitimate minority view that the majority 2 does not fully incorporate — if the Buenos Aires institutional context proves more favorable than global analogues suggest (e.g., the event is explicitly bundled with an ecobici expansion), the impact score could defensibly rise to 3. Second, on feasibility: A3's post-clarification revision to 4 (contingent on an MVP technical pivot) remains an open question — if the team demonstrably adopts the existing-API path and secures even preliminary government and NGO commitments, feasibility could rise to 4. The largest unresolved empirical question across all experts is the behavioral spillover assumption: no evidence is cited for the claim that a one-day awareness walk produces a 10% recycling increase or catalyzes legislative outcomes, and no Buenos Aires-specific longitudinal data exists to ground or refute this. The equity gap identified by A4 and A5 — structural exclusion of informal and shift workers — also remains unquantified in terms of its effect on realistic participation rates and emissions savings.
